docs.title: API Route Organization
docs.text:
Routes in app.py are organized by section (in order):

  1. Page Routes - HTML template rendering (/, /<district>, /<district>/<block>, /<district>/<block>/<gp>)
  2. Block APIs - GET /api/blocks/*
  3. Location APIs - GET /api/locations, /api/districts/*
  4. Protected Areas - GET /api/protected-areas/geojson
  5. Intervention APIs - GET /api/interventions, /api/intervention/<name>/config
  6. Variable APIs - GET /api/variables, /api/variable-groups, /api/variable-stats/*
  7. Feasibility APIs - POST /api/calculate-feasibility, POST /api/gp/calculate-feasibility
  8. AI APIs - POST /api/ai-recommendation, POST /api/ai-recommendation/init
  9. Export API - POST /api/export/csv
  10. Config APIs - GET /api/config, /api/levels
  11. GP APIs - GET /api/gp/*, POST /api/gp/calculate-feasibility
  12. Health & Meta - GET /health, GET /api
  13. Static Serving - /ai-docs/*, /documentation/*
  14. Error Handlers - 404 (returns main app for non-API routes), 500

docs.title: Clustering Workflow
docs.text:
How the LEAF DSS clustering pipeline works end-to-end - from ODK village survey to a finalised cluster consumed by the external production tool. Use this guide as the orientation document for new contributors and for partner handovers.

The workflow was designed in the IWMI requirements call on 2026-04-23 (with Faiz Alam) and the LEAF DSS Clustering Workflow deck. This guide reflects what the API actually implements; the API reference pages document each individual endpoint.

docs.title: 1. The big picture
docs.text:
+-----------------------------------+
ODK / MMUA seed data  ─────►     |  Postgres (Supabase)              |
(villages.csv)                   |    villages.csv source-of-truth   |
        │                        |    clusters table                 |
        │  cluster_block_*()     |    cluster_villages table         |
        ▼                        |    infrastructure table           |
  Greedy seed-and-grow  ─────►   +-----------------------------------+
  (clustering.py)                            │
                                             │  CSV download/upload
                                             │  edit cycle
                                             ▼
                          Block coordinator / IWMI reviewer
                                             │
                                             │  finalize
                                             ▼
                            Outbound feed (production-tool API)
                                             │
                                             ▼
                              External production tool
                                  (auth, per-cluster login,
                                   pashu-sakhi dashboards)
                                             │
                                             │  POST aggregated metrics
                                             ▼
                          /api/production-tool/dashboard/<id>
                                             │
                                             ▼
                            Cluster report card on LEAF DSS UI

docs.title: 2. Input: village-level points
docs.text:
Two sources, same shape:

Source When Status MMUA Random_pointshapefile sheet Pilot bootstrap 275 villages in KHOWANG block, Dibrugarh - currently active. ODK village survey Production Replaces the pilot once collection finishes. Same columns, no API change needed.

Required columns: district_name, block_name, gp_name, vill_name, lat, long, Dairy, Goatery, Piggery, Backyard_Poultry, Duckery, Fishery_Activity.

Each village is a single GPS point (no polygon shapefile needed - design principle from the deck). Member counts are how many SHG members in that village expressed interest in each commodity.

Available via GET /api/villages, GET /api/villages/geojson, GET /api/villages/aggregate.

docs.title: 3. Map levels
docs.text:
The hierarchical map view in the dashboard switches representation as the user drills in. Showing 25k points at state scale would be unreadable, so:

Zoom Endpoint What you see State (Assam) GET /api/villages/aggregate?level=district One choropleth per district (member sums, village count). District GET /api/villages/aggregate?level=block&district=<X> One choropleth per block. Block GET /api/villages/geojson?block=<X> + GET /api/clusters?block=<X>&commodity=<Y> Village points + commodity-filtered cluster overlays.

The block scale is where the real work happens: village dots, optionally tinted by member count, with cluster groupings drawn around them.

docs.title: 4. Clustering - what the algorithm does
docs.text:
Input filter (runs once, before any pass): villages with fewer than min_members_per_village interested members for the commodity are dropped from the candidate set. Every pass below operates on the filtered set.

The pipeline runs five passes. A and B do the main work; C, D, E each address an edge case the earlier passes leave behind.

docs.title: Pass A - Single-village clusters (pre-pass)
docs.text:
Before the greedy loop, any village whose member count for the commodity is already at or above max_cluster_members is emitted as its own one-village cluster. This bypasses both the "minimum 2 villages" rule and the upper member cap.

Why: a 274-member dairy village cannot be paired with any neighbour without busting the 150-member cap, so the greedy loop in Pass B would otherwise drop it. After this pass, large standalone villages stay in the output as singletons.

docs.title: Pass B - Greedy seed-and-grow (main pass)
docs.text:
For the remaining (unassigned) villages:

  1. Sort big-first by member count.
  2. Seed the next cluster with the biggest unassigned village; greedily add nearest neighbours that fit all four constraints: ≤ max_radius_km from the seed, ≤ max_radius_km max pairwise span across the whole trial cluster, total members ≤ max_cluster_members, ≤ max_villages_per_cluster villages.
  3. Discard the cluster if it ends up under floors (< min_cluster_members or < min_villages_per_cluster); release its villages back to the pool.
  4. Repeat for the next seed.

The pairwise-span gate is what prevents a distant village from being stapled onto a cluster just because it's close to the seed — every existing member must also be within max_radius_km.

docs.title: Pass C - Orphan merge (post-pass)
docs.text:
Any village still unassigned after Pass B is merged into the nearest existing cluster whose centroid is within 2 * max_radius_km — but only if every cap still holds after the merge: max_villages_per_cluster, max_cluster_members, AND the recomputed max_pairwise_km ≤ max_radius_km. The earlier loose soft-cap version produced clusters that broke the 5 km span rule (e.g. BARTANGLA at 6 villages / 5.139 km); the current strict re-check rules that out.

The merged cluster keeps its original cluster_id, pashu_sakhi, and block_coordinator; centroid, span, and total are recomputed. Orphans that don't fit any cluster cleanly are left for Pass D rather than corrupting a good cluster.

docs.title: Pass D - Provisional clusters (on by default)
docs.text:
Low-density blocks and pockets leave villages that never clear the min_cluster_members floor (KHOWANG/Dairy is the canonical example — 24 villages, no fundable cluster possible). When emit_provisional is on, each still-unassigned village seeds a provisional cluster using the same growth, radius, and size caps as Pass B, but with the member floor relaxed to provisional_min_members and the 2-village floor dropped so isolated villages still show on the map.

Provisional clusters are flagged with provisional=True in the output. The UI renders them with a dashed amber ring + "Provisional" badge and excludes them from fundable counts, so reviewers can see what's there without confusing them with fundable clusters.

docs.title: Pass E - Rebalance (off by default)
docs.text:
Optional rescue pass. When rebalance=True, a below-floor provisional group can become fundable by borrowing village(s) from an adjacent fundable cluster — but a borrow commits only if the provisional group stays inside all caps AND the donor cluster stays valid (≥ min_cluster_members members, ≥ min_villages_per_cluster villages) after losing the village. If the group can't reach the floor within its remaining village budget, all its tentative borrows are reverted (no churn). Only original fundable clusters may donate, and each village moves at most once — one rescue level, no cascade. Largest provisional groups are processed first; ties are broken deterministically.

Default is off because it changes output across ~20% of provisional groups, which is large enough to need human review before flipping on. Bump ALGO_VERSION when enabling so smart-refresh regenerates every stored scope.

Block boundary is a hard wall - clusters never span two blocks. Each commodity is clustered independently, so one village can sit in many clusters (one per commodity it has interested members in).

Trigger: POST /api/clusters/regenerate with {block, commodity, ...overrides}. Admin-only - pass ?admin=1 or the X-Admin: 1 header. Empty overrides = government defaults (30-150 members, 2-4 villages, 5 km, ≥1 member/village).

Worth noting: a GET on /api/clusters?block=<X>&commodity=<Y> now lazily materialises clusters the first time a (block, commodity) scope is queried, so a fresh block doesn't need an explicit Regenerate to populate.

docs.title: 4a. Worked example - KHOWANG / Goatery / Cluster 1
docs.text:
A concrete cluster from the current pilot data:

Field Value Block KHOWANG (DIBRUGARH) Commodity Goatery cluster_num 1 (sequential, 1..N within the block-commodity scope) cluster_id KHOWANG-Goatery-02b1321b (stable hash; reuse in CSV round-trips) Total members 35 Villages 3 Max pairwise span 1.398 km

The "Why this is a cluster" panel walks through each check using these numbers:

Rule Default range This cluster Outcome Total members in funding band 30 – 150 35 ✓ Village count in 2 – 4 2 – 4 3 ✓ Max pairwise span ≤ radius cap ≤ 5 km 1.398 km ✓ Every village has ≥ 1 interested member ≥ 1 smallest is 8 ✓

Now contrast with two edge cases captured by Passes A and C:

  • Pass A example. If one Goatery village in a block had 274 interested members, Pass B would have rejected it (any neighbour would push the total past 150). Pass A produces a one-village cluster with total_members = 274, village_count = 1, max_span_km = 0. The "Why this is a cluster" panel flags the member-band check as ! rather than ✓ and the comment in the cluster popup notes the single-village exception.
  • Pass C example. A 4-member Goatery village far from any seed is left over by Pass B. Pass C asks: is there a cluster centroid within 2 * 5 = 10 km whose member, village-count, and recomputed pairwise span all still pass after absorbing this village? If yes, append and bump total_members by 4. If no, the village is handed to Pass D, which surfaces it as a provisional cluster (single-village, below the funding floor) so it still appears on the map for review.

The whole cycle is visible in the Cluster Planning workspace - pick KHOWANG, Goatery, hover a ring to see the count, click for the rules panel.

docs.title: 5. Edit cycle (CSV download → edit → re-upload)
docs.text:
Per Faiz's call: "we're not going to give them an interface to change it. The same way we have done our backend data upload thing." The proposed clusters from the algorithm are a starting point - block coordinators can override them based on local knowledge (a mountain between two villages, social tension, road conditions) by editing a CSV and re-uploading.

GET /api/clusters/export.csv?block=KHOWANG&commodity=Goatery
   │   row-per-village CSV with cluster_id, commodity, vill_name, gp_name,
   │   lat, long, members, pashu_sakhi, block_coordinator
   ▼
Open in Excel
   ┌─ split a cluster
   ├─ merge two clusters (use the same cluster_id)
   ├─ drop a village (delete the row)
   ├─ add a brand-new village (append a row with vill_name + lat/long;
   │     coordinates are accepted as-is when the name isn't in the master)
   ├─ fill in pashu_sakhi / block_coordinator
   └─ change member count (rare; e.g. correcting an ODK error)
   │
   ▼
POST /api/clusters/import?block=KHOWANG&commodity=Goatery   (raw CSV body or multipart)
   │   replaces all clusters in scope; recomputes total_members,
   │   max_span_km, centroid_lat/lon from the new village list
   ▼
GET /api/clusters?block=KHOWANG&commodity=Goatery   (verify)

Re-upload as many times as needed. Each upload is a full replace within the scope; partial edits aren't supported (by design - keeps the contract simple and matches the existing LEAF data update flow).

docs.title: 6. Cluster report card
docs.text:
Once a cluster exists, GET /api/clusters/<id>/report returns the cluster plus every LEAF block-level variable for the parent block - soil, water, climate, infrastructure, people, livestock, land/agri (the same dataset that powers /api/blocks).

This mirrors slide 7 of the deck: one summary view per cluster that surfaces all relevant block context. No new variables were added - we re-use existing LEAF data and join on block_name.

docs.title: 7. Infrastructure overlay
docs.text:
POIs (vet centres, pharmacies, input shops, milk-collection centres) live in their own table, uploaded via CSV - same edit-via-CSV pattern as the cluster data. See the Infrastructure API page.

The interesting query is GET /api/infrastructure/nearest?cluster_id=<id>&type=vet_centre&n=3 - returns the closest POIs to a cluster's centroid with distance_km attached. Used on the cluster report card to answer "what's the nearest vet centre for this dairy cluster?"

docs.title: 8. Finalisation and the production-tool handshake
docs.text:
The clustering work is one half of a two-tool integration:

Tool Built by Role LEAF DSS Us Forms clusters, surfaces block context, hands the cluster definition to the production tool. Production tool Partner team (in development) Per-cluster login, day-to-day operations (Pashu Sakhi tracking, output recording), produces aggregate dashboards.

Once cluster edits stabilise, an admin calls POST /api/clusters/<id>/finalize on each cluster. Only finalised clusters appear in the outbound feed GET /api/production-tool/clusters - the contract the production tool consumes.

After the production tool runs for a while, it sends back aggregated metrics via POST /api/production-tool/dashboard/<id>. The payload is an arbitrary JSON blob (e.g. {"period": "2026-Q1", "eggs_produced": 12450, "meat_kg": 320.5}); LEAF DSS stores it as-is and surfaces it on the cluster report card. Schema is intentionally open - the production tool may evolve its output without coordinated releases.

Per the call, the production tool's own user-level data (which Pashu Sakhi did what for which household) stays inside the production tool. We only exchange aggregates.

docs.title: 9. User interface - Cluster Planning workspace
docs.text: The clustering workflow has its own UI surface, separate from the existing block-detail dashboard.

docs.title: URLs
docs.text:
Path When to use /<district>/<block>/clustering Nested under the block detail view. Reached by the "Cluster Planning" button on the block page. Shareable. /clustering/<block> Standalone - same workspace, no district prefix. Useful when you only know the block name. /clustering Standalone, defaults to the first block that has village data ingested. /clustering/<block>?commodity=Goatery Pre-selects a commodity on load.

The "Cluster Planning" button only appears on a block detail page when that block has village data ingested (matched case-insensitively against the Block_name in the LEAF shapefile).

docs.title: What you see
docs.text: Element Purpose Block boundary outline (dark blue stroke, faint teal fill) Spatial context - the hard wall that constrains clustering. Village dots (~275 in Khowang) One per GPS point. Hover → name, member counts per commodity. Grey until a commodity is picked; then interested villages take that commodity's colour and dot size scales with members. District + block dropdowns in the header Switch context without leaving. Only districts/blocks with village data are listed; if the district has just one such block, the dropdown collapses to a static label. Commodity dropdown Pick one of the six commodities. Defaults to "Show villages only". Cluster ring Single neutral-blue containment circle centered on the cluster centroid. Radius bounds all member villages with a small padding. One colour for every cluster; identity comes from the number, not the colour. Overlaps between rings are expected. Cluster numbering Each cluster gets a sequential cluster_num (1, 2, 3...) within the (block, commodity) scope. The hash cluster_id is kept as a faint subtitle for CSV cross-reference. Cluster search pill (toolbar) Type a cluster number or hash. Live border colour for match / no-match; Enter pans, opens the popup, and flashes the ring stroke. Esc clears. Centroid ✓ marker Only on finalised clusters. Proposed clusters have no marker (less visual noise). Cluster popup (on click) Cluster number + ID, members, span, village list, status, Finalise & publish / Unfinalise button. Side panel - cluster summary Members, villages, max span, status, assigned Pashu Sakhi / block coordinator, the full village list, Other commodities in these villages (aggregated from the same village set), and Why this is a cluster with a tick or ! per rule. Toolbar - Map key Decodes the ring, village dot size, and the ✓ marker. Toolbar - Download CSV / Upload CSV The edit cycle for the current scope (block + commodity). Toolbar - Regenerate Admin-only. Hidden by default; appears when ?admin=1 is in the URL or localStorage.leaf_admin=1. The POST endpoint itself returns 403 without the same flag. ? button in the header Opens the multilingual workflow help (EN / हिं / অস). ? button inside cluster popups Opens the same workflow help overlay (so the popup itself stays compact).

docs.title: Multilingual help
docs.text: The ? overlay has tabs for English, Hindi (हिं), and Assamese (অস), with a 6-step walkthrough of the whole workspace and a yellow caveat box noting that the Hindi/Assamese strings are AI-drafted and need a native-speaker review before final rollout. Source strings live in leaf_flask/static/js/clusters.js under WORKFLOW_HELP.

docs.title: Loaders
docs.text: Async actions (cluster fetch, village load, regenerate, upload, finalise) show inline spinners in the toolbar summary line so the user gets feedback during slow Postgres round-trips on Render free tier.

docs.title: 10. Persistence and deployment
docs.text: Concern Approach Cluster store Postgres on Supabase. Tables: clusters, cluster_villages (cascading delete), infrastructure. Schema in leaf_flask/schema.sql. Village seed Read-only CSV at leaf_flask/data/villages.csv (committed). Replaced by ODK feed in production. Connection pooling psycopg2.pool.ThreadedConnectionPool (1-10 conns) in leaf_flask/db.py. Env vars DATABASE_URL, SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY, SUPABASE_SECRET_KEY. Loaded from .env locally; set in the Render dashboard for production. Why a DB Render's free disk is ephemeral - file-based persistence (clusters.json) reset on every redeploy. Postgres survives.

docs.title: 11. Configurable parameters
docs.text:
All four cluster constraints are server-side defaults but request-time overridable via the regenerate endpoint:

Parameter Default Source min_members_per_village 1 LEAF min_cluster_members 30 Government max_cluster_members 150 Government min_villages_per_cluster 2 Government max_villages_per_cluster 4 Government max_radius_km 5.0 Government

Government numbers come from the budgeting rule: each cluster will be allocated one trillion (recurring grant) so the band has a hard floor and ceiling. Faiz expects to iterate on these before final freeze.

docs.title: 12. What's not yet implemented
docs.text:
Tracked for follow-up:

  • State and district choropleth UI: backend /api/villages/aggregate is live but the top-level dashboard map doesn't paint districts/blocks by member counts yet. The Cluster Planning workspace itself is at block scale and doesn't need it.
  • Authentication on the production-tool endpoints: currently open. Should add an API key header before the partner team integrates live.
  • Native-speaker review of Hindi / Assamese help copy: the WORKFLOW_HELP strings in clusters.js are AI-drafted and flagged with a translation caveat in the help overlay. Replace with reviewed copy before final rollout.
  • Parameter-tuning UI: the four cluster constraints (member band, village count, radius) are tunable via the API only; the Regenerate button uses defaults. Add an inline form when users hit "0 clusters formed" (e.g. for Dairy in Khowang).
  • PostGIS / earthdistance: nearest-N infrastructure query is currently in-Python haversine. Acceptable at current POI volumes; switch when the dataset grows beyond a few thousand rows or when query latency becomes a concern.
  • Persistent disk on Render: cluster + POI tables are in Supabase Postgres which survives redeploys, but Render's local disk is still ephemeral - anything written to data/ is regenerated rather than persisted. Fine today; revisit if we add file uploads.
  • AI advisory layer: slide 9 of the deck. Out of scope for the first iteration - revisit after the cluster pipeline is live.

docs.title: 13. Reference implementation
docs.text: File Purpose clustering.py Pure-Python algorithm; no Flask/DB dependency. villages.py Village seed loader + Postgres-backed cluster store. infrastructure.py POI store + nearest-N query. db.py Postgres connection pool. schema.sql Idempotent DDL - runs once against a fresh Supabase project. app.py All HTTP routes with Swagger YAML docstrings. templates/_cluster_planner.html Shared workspace HTML used by both the modal (index.html) and the page (clustering.html). static/js/clusters.js All workspace logic - map setup, dropdowns, popups, CSV cycle, finalise, multilingual help.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text: LEAF DSS can be deployed to cloud platforms (Render recommended) or run locally for development.

docs.title: Render (Production)
docs.text: The app is deployed on Render as a Web Service.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Setting Value Runtime Python Build Command pip install -r leaf_flask/requirements.txt && pip install mkdocs-material && mkdocs build Start Command cd leaf_flask && gunicorn app:app --bind 0.0.0.0:$PORT Plan Free or Starter

Build Command

The build command installs MkDocs and builds the documentation site into site/, which Flask then serves at /documentation/. If you don't need the docs site, simplify to just pip install -r leaf_flask/requirements.txt.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Set these in the Render dashboard under Environment:

Variable Required Description SECRET_KEY Yes Flask secret key (generate a random string) OPENAI_API_KEY No Required only for AI recommendations PYTHON_VERSION No Python version (e.g. 3.11.0)

docs.title: Deploy Steps
docs.text:
1. Push code to GitHub
  2. Create a new Web Service in Render dashboard
  3. Connect your GitHub repository
  4. Set the Build and Start commands as above
  5. Add environment variables
  6. Click Deploy

Render auto-deploys on every push to the main branch.

docs.title: Health Checks
docs.text:
Configure Render's health check to use: 

GET /health

docs.title: Available URLs (Production)
docs.text: URL Description https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/ Main dashboard (map UI) https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/docs Swagger API documentation https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/documentation/ MkDocs written documentation https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api API endpoint listing https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/health Health check

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
cd leaf_flask
pip install -r requirements.txt
python app.py

Runs on http://localhost:5000 with debug mode and auto-reload enabled.

docs.title: With Documentation Site
docs.text:
# From project root
pip install mkdocs-material
mkdocs build                    # Generates site/ directory
cd leaf_flask
python app.py                   # Serves docs at /documentation/

docs.title: MkDocs Live Preview
docs.text:
For editing documentation with live preview:

mkdocs serve                    # Serves at http://localhost:8000

This runs a separate MkDocs dev server with hot-reload - changes to docs/*.md reflect instantly.

docs.title: Documentation Site
docs.text: The MkDocs documentation is served directly from the Flask app at /documentation/. This means both the API and its documentation are available from the same URL.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. mkdocs build generates a static HTML site in site/
  2. Flask serves these files via the /documentation/ route
  3. MkDocs is configured with site_url: https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/documentation/ so all internal links resolve correctly

docs.title: Rebuilding Documentation
docs.text:
After editing any file in docs/:

mkdocs build          # Regenerate site/ directory

The Flask app serves from site/ - no restart needed (files are served dynamically).

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
MkDocs configuration is in mkdocs.yml at the project root:

Setting Value Purpose site_url https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/documentation/ Ensures internal links work under /documentation/ theme.name material Material for MkDocs theme plugins search Full-text search in documentation

docs.title: File Requirements
docs.text: Ensure these data files are present in leaf_flask/data/:

docs.title: Required (core functionality)
docs.text: File(s) Description 4DSS_VAR_2.0.shp + .dbf, .shx, .prj Block-level shapefile with indicator data Block_assam.shp + .dbf, .shx, .prj District-block mapping shapefile DSS_input2.csv Variable metadata and intervention config

docs.title: Optional (enhanced features)
docs.text: File(s) Description Feature grampanchayat.shp + .dbf, .shx, .prj GP boundaries (Tinsukia) GP-level views Tinsukia_GP_data.xlsx GP indicator data GP variables Block_GPs.xlsx GP-to-block mapping GP block grouping Vill_Gp_join.csv Village-GP mapping GP code resolution districts.geojson District boundaries Map overlay protected_areas/Protected_Area_India_Final.shp + .dbf, .shx, .prj, .cst Protected Areas of India Map overlay

docs.text: File(s) Description ai-docs/*.pdf Policy documents for RAG pipeline

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution App won't start Check pip install -r leaf_flask/requirements.txt completed without errors Shapefile not found Verify .shp, .dbf, .shx, and .prj files all exist in leaf_flask/data/ AI returns 503 Set OPENAI_API_KEY env var and call POST /api/ai-recommendation/init Docs return 404 Run mkdocs build from the project root to generate the site/ directory Slow first request Normal - first request loads and caches all shapefiles (~3-5 seconds) GP data missing GP data only exists for Tinsukia - ensure GP shapefiles and Excel files are present Render deploy fails Check build logs - most common issue is missing Python version or system dependencies

docs.title: Hosting & Handover Guide
docs.text: This guide is for anyone who needs to host LEAF DSS independently - whether you're a government IT team, an NGO partner, a research institution, or a developer taking over the project. It covers what you need, your hosting options, estimated costs, and step-by-step instructions.

docs.title: What You're Hosting
docs.text: LEAF DSS is a single Python web application that serves both the interactive map UI and the REST API. There is no separate frontend deployment - everything runs from one process.

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text:
Resource Minimum Recommended CPU 1 vCPU 2 vCPUs RAM 512 MB 1-2 GB Disk 500 MB 1 GB Python 3.10+ 3.11 or 3.12 OS Any Linux Ubuntu 22.04+

RAM is the main constraint - the app loads shapefiles into memory (~10-15 MB cached). With the AI/RAG feature enabled, RAM usage increases to ~500 MB due to ChromaDB and embeddings.

docs.title: What's Included
docs.text: Component Description Required? Flask web app Map UI + REST API + Swagger docs Yes Shapefiles & CSV Block/GP/district/protected area geodata Yes MkDocs site Written documentation (pre-built HTML) Optional AI/RAG pipeline Policy document recommendations Optional PDF policy docs Source documents for AI Only if AI enabled

docs.title: Option 1: Render (Current Setup) - Recommended for Getting Started
docs.text:
Render is a managed platform that auto-deploys from GitHub.

Plan Monthly Cost RAM CPU Notes Free $0 512 MB Shared Sleeps after 15 min inactivity, cold starts ~30s Starter $7/month 512 MB Shared Always on, no sleep Standard $25/month 2 GB 1 vCPU Recommended for production Pro $85/month 4 GB 2 vCPUs For heavy AI/RAG usage

Pros: Zero server management, auto-deploy from Git, free SSL, health checks. Cons: Free tier has cold starts; US/EU regions only.

docs.title: Setup Steps
docs.text:
1. Push code to a GitHub repository
  2. Sign up at render.com
  3. Click New → Web Service → connect your GitHub repo
  4. Configure:
    • Build Command: pip install -r leaf_flask/requirements.txt && pip install mkdocs-material && mkdocs build
    • Start Command: cd leaf_flask && gunicorn app:app --bind 0.0.0.0:$PORT --workers 2 --timeout 120
  5. Add environment variables:
    • SECRET_KEY = any random string
    • OPENAI_API_KEY = your OpenAI key (only if using AI features)
  6. Click Deploy

docs.title: Option 2: AWS (EC2 + Elastic Beanstalk or ECS)
docs.text:
Best for organizations already on AWS with IT teams.

Service Monthly Cost (estimate) Notes EC2 t3.micro ~$8/month 1 vCPU, 1 GB RAM, free tier eligible (1 year) EC2 t3.small ~$15/month 2 vCPU, 2 GB RAM - recommended Elastic Beanstalk EC2 cost + $0 Managed deployment wrapper around EC2 ECS Fargate ~$15-30/month Serverless containers, no EC2 management Application Load Balancer ~$16/month Add if you need custom domain + SSL Route 53 (DNS) ~$0.50/month Custom domain routing

Total estimate: $15-50/month for a basic production setup.

docs.title: Quick EC2 Setup
docs.text:
# On a fresh Ubuntu 22.04 EC2 instance
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y python3-pip python3-venv nginx

# Clone and setup
git clone <repo-url> /opt/iwmi-leaf
cd /opt/iwmi-leaf/leaf_flask
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
pip install mkdocs-material
cd .. && mkdocs build && cd leaf_flask

# Run with Gunicorn
gunicorn app:app --bind 0.0.0.0:5000 --workers 2 --timeout 120 --daemon

# (Optional) Set up Nginx reverse proxy for port 80/443

Use a process manager

In production, use systemd or supervisord to keep Gunicorn running and auto-restart on crashes. See the systemd section below.

docs.title: Option 3: Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
docs.text:
Service Monthly Cost (estimate) Notes Cloud Run ~$5-15/month Serverless containers, pay per request, auto-scaling Compute Engine e2-small ~$13/month 2 vCPU, 2 GB RAM App Engine (Standard) ~$0-25/month Managed platform, auto-scales to zero

Cloud Run is the most cost-effective for low-to-moderate traffic.

docs.title: Cloud Run Setup
docs.text:
# Build container
docker build -t iwmi-leaf .
docker tag iwmi-leaf gcr.io/YOUR_PROJECT/iwmi-leaf
docker push gcr.io/YOUR_PROJECT/iwmi-leaf

# Deploy
gcloud run deploy iwmi-leaf \
  --image gcr.io/YOUR_PROJECT/iwmi-leaf \
  --port 5000 \
  --memory 1Gi \
  --cpu 1 \
  --allow-unauthenticated

docs.title: Option 4: Azure
docs.text: Service Monthly Cost (estimate) Notes App Service B1 ~$13/month 1 vCPU, 1.75 GB RAM App Service B2 ~$26/month 2 vCPU, 3.5 GB RAM Container Instances ~$10-20/month Serverless containers

docs.title: Option 5: DigitalOcean / Hetzner (Budget VPS)
docs.text:
Best value for money if you're comfortable with basic Linux administration.

Provider Plan Monthly Cost Specs DigitalOcean Basic Droplet $6/month 1 vCPU, 1 GB RAM, 25 GB SSD DigitalOcean Regular Droplet $12/month 2 vCPU, 2 GB RAM, 50 GB SSD Hetzner CX22 €4/month (~$4.50) 2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM, 40 GB - best value DigitalOcean App Platform Basic $5/month Managed platform (like Render)

docs.title: Option 6: On-Premises / Government Data Center
docs.text:
For government agencies or organizations with internal hosting infrastructure.

Requirement Details Server Any Linux server with Python 3.10+, 2 GB RAM Network HTTP/HTTPS access on ports 80/443 SSL Use Let's Encrypt (free) or organization's certificate Backup Only the data/ directory and ai-docs/ need backup Updates Pull from Git and restart the service

docs.title: Cost Summary
docs.text:
Scenario Monthly Cost Best For Free / testing $0 Demos, evaluation (Render free tier) Small production $5-15/month Low traffic, internal use Standard production $15-30/month Public-facing, reliable uptime With AI features $30-50/month Includes OpenAI API usage (~$5-20/month extra) On-premises $0 (infra cost) Government/institutional hosting

OpenAI API Costs

The AI recommendation feature calls the OpenAI API per request. Typical cost is $0.01-0.05 per recommendation. With 100 recommendations/month, expect ~$1-5/month in API fees on top of hosting costs. You can also use a self-hosted LLM to avoid this cost entirely.

docs.title: Handover Checklist
docs.text: If you're handing this project to another team, ensure they have:

docs.title: 1. Source Code
docs.text:
• [ ] Access to the Git repository (GitHub/GitLab)
  • [ ] All branches up to date

docs.title: 2. Data Files
docs.text:
These are NOT in Git (too large). Transfer them separately:

  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/4DSS_VAR_2.0.* (block shapefile - .shp, .dbf, .shx, .prj, .qmd)
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/Block_assam.* (district mapping shapefile)
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/grampanchayat.* (GP shapefile)
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/districts.geojson
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/protected_areas/Protected_Area_India_Final.*
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/DSS_input2.csv
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/Tinsukia_GP_data.xlsx
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/Block_GPs.xlsx
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/Vill_Gp_join.csv
  • [ ] ai-docs/*.pdf (policy documents for AI)

docs.title: 3. Environment Variables
docs.text:
• [ ] SECRET_KEY - any random string for Flask sessions
  • [ ] OPENAI_API_KEY - only if using AI recommendation feature

docs.title: 4. Documentation
docs.text:
• [ ] This documentation site (auto-generated from docs/)
  • [ ] Swagger API docs (auto-generated at /docs)

docs.title: 5. Knowledge Transfer
docs.text:
• [ ] How to add new interventions (edit DSS_input2.csv)
  • [ ] How to update shapefiles (replace files in data/)
  • [ ] How to add new AI documents (add PDFs to ai-docs/, call /api/ai-recommendation/init)
  • [ ] How to add GP data for new districts (new shapefile + Excel + mapping)

docs.title: Systemd Service (Linux)
docs.text:
Create /etc/systemd/system/iwmi-leaf.service:

[Unit]
Description=LEAF DSS Flask Application
After=network.target

[Service]
User=www-data
WorkingDirectory=/opt/iwmi-leaf/leaf_flask
Environment="SECRET_KEY=your-secret-key-here"
Environment="OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-..."
ExecStart=/opt/iwmi-leaf/leaf_flask/venv/bin/gunicorn app:app \
    --bind 127.0.0.1:5000 \
    --workers 2 \
    --timeout 120 \
    --access-logfile /var/log/iwmi-leaf/access.log \
    --error-logfile /var/log/iwmi-leaf/error.log
Restart=always
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

sudo mkdir -p /var/log/iwmi-leaf
sudo systemctl enable iwmi-leaf
sudo systemctl start iwmi-leaf
sudo systemctl status iwmi-leaf

docs.title: Nginx Reverse Proxy
docs.text:
server {
    listen 80;
    server_name iwmi-leaf.yourdomain.com;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:5000;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
        proxy_read_timeout 120s;
    }

    # Optional: serve static files directly for better performance
    location /static/ {
        alias /opt/iwmi-leaf/leaf_flask/static/;
        expires 7d;
    }
}

Then enable HTTPS with Let's Encrypt:

sudo apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx
sudo certbot --nginx -d iwmi-leaf.yourdomain.com

docs.title: Docker (Optional)
docs.text:
Create a Dockerfile in the project root:

FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies for GeoPandas
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    libgdal-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Python dependencies
COPY leaf_flask/requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt gunicorn

# Copy application
COPY leaf_flask/ ./leaf_flask/
COPY site/ ./site/
COPY ai-docs/ ./ai-docs/

WORKDIR /app/leaf_flask

EXPOSE 5000

CMD ["gunicorn", "app:app", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:5000", "--workers", "2", "--timeout", "120"]

docker build -t iwmi-leaf .
docker run -p 5000:5000 -e SECRET_KEY=mysecret iwmi-leaf

docs.title: Routine Updates
docs.text:
cd /opt/iwmi-leaf
git pull origin main
cd leaf_flask
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt   # Only if dependencies changed
cd .. && mkdocs build && cd leaf_flask  # Only if docs changed
sudo systemctl restart iwmi-leaf

docs.title: Updating Data
docs.text:
1. Replace the relevant files in leaf_flask/data/
  2. Restart the service - the LRU cache clears on restart
  3. No code changes needed

docs.title: Adding a New District's GP Data
docs.text:
1. Prepare a GP-level shapefile (.shp + sidecar files)
  2. Prepare a GP indicator Excel file (same column format as Tinsukia_GP_data.xlsx)
  3. Update data_utils.py to load the new files
  4. Update the gp_districts list in the /api/levels route
  5. Restart the service

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text: Area Current State Recommendation for Production Authentication None (open API) Add API key or OAuth if needed HTTPS Handled by Render/Nginx Always use HTTPS in production Secret key Default in code Set via environment variable CORS Open to all origins Restrict to your domain if needed Rate limiting None Add Flask-Limiter if public-facing Input validation Basic Sufficient for current use File uploads None No risk SQL injection N/A (no database) No risk
